title: Draught-stripping a front door
subtitle: Seal the frame, the threshold, and the letterbox in an afternoon
excerpt: A draughty front door leaks warm air through gaps in the frame, the threshold, and the letterbox. Fitting foam strip, a threshold seal, and a letterbox brush takes two to three hours. The payback is inside the first heating season.
sourceNotes: Energy Saving Trust draught-proofing guidance (energysavingtrust.org.uk, accessed May 2026). EST estimate: draught-proofing a house produces measurable savings on a typical UK gas tariff. gov.uk Building Regulations Part F (ventilation) for background on intentional vs unintentional ventilation.

recipeTools.notes: For cutting foam strip and threshold seal to length.

glossaryTerms.definition: Self-adhesive foam, brush, or rubber strips applied to the meeting surfaces of doors, windows, lofts, and floor gaps to stop air infiltration. The fastest-payback retrofit; a £20 kit cuts measurable heat loss in any uninsulated room.

content.text: Cold air moves through a front-door frame in three distinct paths: the sides and top (where door meets frame), the threshold (where door meets floor), and the letterbox slot. Fixing all three at once is the correct approach; sealing only the frame while leaving the threshold open makes a detectable but incomplete improvement.
Draught stripping

a front door on a typical UK semi saves around 3-6% of the heat lost through the building envelope from this path alone. The Energy Saving Trust estimates whole-house draught-proofing saves measurably on the annual gas bill at current rates. The materials for a single door are inexpensive; payback is inside the first heating season.
What you need
Self-adhesive foam or rubber draught strip, measure the perimeter of the door frame (top + two sides) and buy 10% extra for waste. P-profile or D-profile foam works on most standard UK rebated doors.
Threshold seal, a brush strip screwed to the door bottom, or an automatic drop seal if you have a carpeted threshold. Measure the door width.
Letterbox brush insert, a nylon-brush plate that fits behind the letterbox.
White spirit or acetone, clean cloth, for degreasing the door frame before the foam strip goes on.
Crosshead screwdriver, for the letterbox brush and threshold seal screws.
Preparation

Check that the door closes properly. If the door drops or sticks, fix that first: draught strip adds 2-3 mm of compression and a sticking door becomes a non-closing door. Check the frame for rot at the sill, any soft timber needs treating before the seal goes over it.
Frame sealing
Step 1, clean the frame rebate.
Wipe the rebate (the inset step the door closes against) with white spirit on a clean cloth. Let it dry completely. The adhesive won't hold on a greasy or dusty surface.
Step 2, apply the foam strip.

Peel the backing and press P-profile or D-profile foam strip into the rebate along the top of the frame and both sides. Start at the top, press firmly along the full length, and cut cleanly with a sharp knife at the corners. The foam should be compressed by about half when the door closes.
Step 3, check the door closes without binding.

Close the door slowly. You should feel resistance as the foam compresses but the door should latch without forcing. If it won't close, the foam profile is too large for the gap: use a thinner profile or take one or two passes off the timber rebate with a block plane.
Threshold seal
Step 4, measure and cut.
Measure the door width at the bottom. Cut the threshold brush seal to length with a hacksaw or strong scissors. Most brush seals come with an aluminium carrier that can be cut with a hacksaw.
Step 5, fix the seal.

Hold the seal against the door bottom so the bristles just brush the threshold (not so tight they prevent the door closing). Mark the screw positions with a pencil, pre-drill if needed, and fix with the supplied screws. Close the door and check for drag.
Letterbox seal
Step 6, fit the letterbox brush.

Remove the inner letterbox plate if there is one. Feed the brush insert into the slot from inside, aligning the screw holes with the existing plate holes. Tighten the screws so the brushes sit squarely across the opening. Replace the inner plate if using a plate-and-brush combination kit.
What to check

On a cold day, hold the back of your hand close to the frame perimeter with the door closed. Any remaining draught shows as a cool current. Check the hinge side: this is the most commonly missed gap, especially at the top and bottom hinge positions where the door has been planed at some point and the rebate is wider.

attrs.title: Ventilation note
attrs.body: Draught-proofing a door reduces uncontrolled air infiltration, not ventilation. If the house is well sealed, make sure trickle vents on windows are open and bathroom and kitchen extract fans are working. Part F of the Building Regulations covers minimum ventilation, the guidance is free on gov.uk.

content.text: Troubleshooting

items.symptom: Foam strip peels off within a week
items.cause: Frame was not degreased before fitting, or temperature was below 5°C when applied
items.fix: Remove, clean thoroughly with acetone, let dry fully, apply new strip at room temperature.

items.symptom: Door won't close after fitting foam
items.cause: Foam profile too thick for the rebate gap
items.fix: Use a thinner P-profile; or plane 1-2 mm off the rebate with a block plane.

items.symptom: Letterbox still draughty after brush install
items.cause: Brush bristles are not fully spanning the opening, or the box has a worn-out outer flap
items.fix: Ensure bristles press flat across the full slot width; replace outer flap if badly worn.
